article_title: Katalyst Wraps Environmental Testing of LINK Servicer Clearing the Way for a June Pegasus XL Launch to Save NASA's Swift Observatory

content_preview.title: Katalyst Wraps Environmental Testing of LINK Servicer Clearing the Way for a June Pegasus XL Launch to Save NASA's Swift Observatory
content_preview.summary: Katalyst Space Technologies finished thermal-vacuum and vibration testing of its LINK servicing spacecraft at NASA Goddard on May 4, 2026, on track for a June Pegasus XL launch to raise the Swift Observatory's decaying orbit.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Katalyst Space Technologies has completed environmental testing of its LINK robotic servicing spacecraft at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Maryland, clearing the most demanding pre-launch gate for a June 2026 rendezvous with the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory. NASA confirmed the milestone on May 8, four days after the final test wrapped, according to the agency's [Swift mission blog](https://science.nasa.gov/blogs/swift/2026/05/08/katalyst-wraps-testing-at-nasa-goddard-for-sw...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong 861-word News piece. Clear scope (testing completion → June launch → Swift boost), explicit framing of risk trade-off, structured 'What We Don't Know'.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 3 snapshots from disk. NASA Swift blog (source-0) confirms May 8 announcement of May 4 test completion, $30M contract, September 2025 awarded, 3 ion thrusters, Kieran Wilson and Van Eepoel attributions, 27-foot Space Environment Simulator, eight-month timeline, Flagstaff/Broomfield locations. Spaceflight Now (source-1) confirms $30M contract, $500M Swift, 600km→400km altitude drop, eight months, 20.6-degree inclination, Marshall Islands launch, Pegasus XL. Space.com (source-2) confirms 4.9 feet / 1.5m / 770 pounds / 350 kg dimensions, 3 robotic arms, 22-year extension, Hubble servicing reference, Ghonhee Lee 'only launch vehicle' quote. All three direct quotes (Wilson 'unusual situation where the schedule', Van Eepoel 'fast, high-risk, high-reward', Lee 'only launch vehicle that can meet the orbit') verified verbatim in their cited sources.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific (dollar figures, dates, dimensions, dimensions, inclination, locations, executive titles) traces to its cited authority. Multi-source corroboration on major facts.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and procedural. 'Fast, high-risk, high-reward' framing properly attributed to NASA's mission director; the article does not editorialize on the outcome.
editor_notes.originality: No archive collision. Genuinely new milestone for an unfolding mission.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Clean APPROVE. Multi-sourced, all direct quotes verified verbatim, careful framing of an unusual contracting model and a non-cooperative-rendezvous mission.
